questions.question: As elements get heavier, the neutron-to-proton ratio required for a stable nucleus:
options: Rises toward roughly 1.5 neutrons per proton
questions.explanation: Light nuclides are stable near N:Z = 1:1, but heavier nuclei need extra neutrons to dilute the growing proton repulsion - the band of stability bends up toward about 1.5:1 (e.g., Pb-208 with 126 n / 82 p).

questions.question: Stable selenium-74 is irradiated in a reactor and captures one neutron. The product is:
options: Selenium-75, a radioisotope of the same element
questions.explanation: Neutron activation raises the mass number by one and leaves the element unchanged: Se-74 + n → Se-75, the low-energy gamma source used for thin-wall work.

questions.question: Cesium-137 used in sealed sources is obtained by:
options: Neutron activation of stable cesium-136 targets
Chemical separation from spent reactor fuel, where it accumulates as a fission product
questions.explanation: Cs-137 is a fission product: U-235 fragments are born neutron-rich and radioactive, and Cs-137 is recovered chemically from spent fuel rather than made by activation.

questions.question: A source certificate lists 2 Ci. In SI units this is:
questions.explanation: 1 Ci = 3.7 × 10¹⁰ dps = 37 GBq, so 2 Ci = 74 GBq. Field gamma sources fall in the gigabecquerel-to-terabecquerel range.

questions.question: A Co-60 source (half-life ≈ 5.27 years) is assayed at 60 Ci. About 10.5 years later - two half-lives - its activity is:
questions.explanation: Halve once per half-life: 60 → 30 → 15 Ci after two half-lives. Activity never reaches zero on paper; it only keeps halving.

questions.question: A qualified technique required 3 minutes with the Ir-192 source at 90 Ci. The decay chart shows 45 Ci today. The exposure time should be:
options: 3 minutes - the technique is qualified as written
questions.explanation: Exposure in curie-minutes must stay constant, so time scales inversely with activity: 3 min × (90/45) = 6 minutes. Skipping the decay correction produces underexposed film.

questions.question: A beta particle is best described as:
options: A helium nucleus ejected from heavy atoms
An electron ejected from the nucleus when a neutron converts to a proton
An orbital electron knocked loose by a photon
A neutral particle with the mass of a proton
questions.explanation: Beta decay converts a neutron into a proton and ejects an electron from the nucleus - exactly how neutron-rich activation products like Ir-192 move back toward stability. The source capsule absorbs these betas.

questions.question: Which radiation type is stopped by a single sheet of paper or the dead outer layer of skin?
questions.explanation: The alpha's large mass and +2 charge make it intensely ionizing but extremely short-ranged - an inch or two of air, paper, or dead skin stops it. Its hazard is internal exposure, not external.

questions.question: Raising the kilovoltage on an X-ray machine while leaving mA unchanged will:
options: Increase the number of photons without changing their energy
Raise the maximum photon energy and shift the spectrum upward, increasing penetration
Narrow the spectrum to a single discrete line
questions.explanation: kV sets how hard each electron strikes the target, which fixes the spectrum's upper limit and shifts its bulk to higher energies - the penetration/quality control. mA controls only quantity.

questions.question: One curie of Co-60 compared with one curie of Ir-192 at one meter produces:
options: The same dose rate, because the activities are equal
A lower dose rate, because cobalt decays more slowly
Roughly 1.3 R/h versus roughly 0.5 R/h - more than double, because each Co-60 decay emits two high-energy gammas
Exactly ten times the dose rate
questions.explanation: Activity counts decays, not emitted energy. Co-60 throws two energetic photons (1.17 and 1.33 MeV) per decay, giving about 1.3 R/h per Ci at 1 m versus about 0.5 for Ir-192 - same curies, very different field.
